retrieved_docs.title: Phagocytosis -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: A large spectrum of particles can be sequestered through phagocytosis. Thus, the process is heavily utilized by the human host immune systems – whether host macrophages are clearing dead cell debris or capturing bacterial pathogens with the help of opsonins. Many cell types are capable of performing phagocytic endocytosis in the body; however, certain cells are specialized to primarily perform phagocytosis. For example, dendritic cells capture bacteria, digest, and subsequently present bacterial component proteins to helper T-cells. The initiation of helper T-cell division by dendritic cells is just one of many instances phagocytosis linking the innate and adaptive immune responses. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Alveolar Macrophages -- Function -- Phagocytosis and Immune Defense
retrieved_docs.content: The primary function of alveolar macrophages is phagocytosis, which involves engulfing and digesting pathogens, apoptotic cells, and particulate matter entering the lungs through inhalation. Alveolar macrophages act as the first line of defense in the innate immune system by detecting and neutralizing foreign materials, including bacteria, viruses, and pollutants.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Immune Response -- Function -- Immune Response to Bacteria
retrieved_docs.content: Neutralizing antibodies are produced if the bacterial pathogenicity is due to a toxin. Opsonizing antibodies are produced as they are essential in destroying extracellular bacteria. The complement system is activated especially by gram-negative bacterial lipid layers. Phagocytes kill most bacteria by using positive chemotaxis, attachment, uptake, and, finally, engulfing the bacteria. CD8+ T cells can kill cells infected by bacteria. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Phagocytosis -- Review Questions
retrieved_docs.content: Access free multiple choice questions on this topic. Comment on this article.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Immune Response -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: Macrophages produce lysosomal enzymes and reactive oxygen species to eliminate the ingested pathogens. These cells produce cytokines that attract other leukocytes to the site of infection. The innate response to viruses includes synthesizing and releasing interferons and activating natural killer cells that recognize and destroy the virus-infected cells. The innate immunity against bacteria consists of activating neutrophils that ingest pathogens and moving monocytes to the inflamed tissue, where they become macrophages. These macrophages can engulf and process the antigen and present it to activated specialized cells. Eosinophils protect against parasitic infections by releasing the content of their granules. Antibody-dependent cell-mediated cytotoxicity: A cytotoxic reaction where Fc-receptor-expressing killer cells recognize target cells through specific antibodies. Affinity maturation: The increase in average antibody affinity is mostly observed during a secondary immune response.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Opsonization -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: Opsonization is an immune process which uses opsonins to tag foreign pathogens for elimination by phagocytes. Without an opsonin, such as an antibody, the negatively-charged cell walls of the pathogen and phagocyte repel each other. The pathogen can then avoid destruction and continue to replicate inside the human body.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Immune Response -- Function -- Strategies of Bacteria to Evade the Immune System
retrieved_docs.content: Intracellular pathogens may hide in cells: Bacteria can live inside metabolically damaged host leukocytes and escape from phagolysosomes ( Shigella spp ). Production of toxins that inhibit the phagocytosis. They prevent killing by encapsulation. The release of catalase inactivates hydrogen peroxide. They infect cells and then cause impaired antigenic presentation. The organism may kill the phagocyte by apoptosis or necrosis.

retrieved_docs.title: Scrofula (Archived) -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Most of the cases of peripheral lymphadenopathy are believed to have resulted from the reactivation of latent tuberculosis. [6] Tubercle bacilli are either infected through droplets or ingested through the milk, mainly mycobacterium bovis. Cell-mediated immunity is responsible for the resistance against the mycobacterium. Pathogenicity depends upon the host factors (genetic and acquired susceptibilities), the virulence of bacteria, metabolic factors for the intracellular survival of bacteria. If the innate immunity of the host fails to defeat the infection, microorganisms proliferate within alveolar macrophages and disseminate away from the lungs to peripheral tissue. Progressive primary TB drives massive hematogenous dissemination, mainly to cervical lymph nodes. Macrophages play a central role in host cell immunity against mycobacterium. Once the mycobacteria enter and reach the macrophages endosomes, macrophage-mannose receptors and complement receptors recognize and bind several components of bacteria such as mannose-capped glycolipid mycobacterium inhibits the phagolysosome formation that permits unchecked mycobacterial proliferation. This indicates the pre-sensitized phase in the first 3 weeks. This ensues bacteremia, and bacteria invade many peripheral tissues, mostly lymph nodes.

retrieved_docs.title: Phagocytosis -- Issues of Concern -- Receptor-led Formation of the Phagosome
retrieved_docs.content: As the phagosome traverses in the direction of the phagocyte’s centrosome, it fuses with a lysosome containing degradative enzymes such as proteases and iron-sequestering molecules such as lactoferrin. This fused organelle is termed the phagolysosome and gradually acidifies to activate proteolytic and degradative enzymes. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Phagocytosis -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: In phagocytosis, the plasma membrane of a cell is directed by cytoskeletal filaments to form pseudopodia (fake arms) that act to engulf a particle and bring it into the cell from the extracellular matrix. Once engulfed, the particle remains compartmentalized in an intracellular vesicle known as a phagosome and eventually undergoes lysosomal degradation in the phagocytic pathway. Phagocytosis is primarily a eukaryotic process. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Opsonization -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: Opsonins are used to overcome the repellent force between the negative cell walls and promote uptake of the pathogen by the macrophage. Opsonization is an antimicrobial technique to kill and stop the spread of disease.

retrieved_docs.title: Ehrlichiosis -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Infection with Ehrlichia species begins with the intracellular uptake of the infectious extracellular form of the organism, the elementary body or dense core. The elementary body or dense core is then taken up by endocytosis, where the organism replicates and matures to form a reticulate body or reticulate core and then morula before redifferentiating into an elementary body/dense core that leaves the infected host cell to spread infection. [32] During this process, Ehrlichia uses several immune evasion mechanisms, including suppression of host cell apoptosis, modulation of chemokine and cytokine responses, and downregulation of host pattern recognition receptors that might enable clearance of the infection.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Immune Response -- Function -- Strategies of Fungi to Evade the Immune System
retrieved_docs.content: Fungi produce a polysaccharide capsule, which inhibits phagocytosis and overcomes opsonization, complement, and antibodies. Some fungi inhibit the activities of host T cells from delaying cell-mediated killing. Other organisms, such as Histoplasma capsulatum , evade macrophage killing by entering the cells through CR3 and escaping from the phagosome. Strategies of Parasites to Evade the Immune System Parasites can resist destruction by complement. Intracellular parasites can avoid being killed by lysosomal enzymes and oxygen metabolites. Parasites disguise themselves as a protection mechanism. Antigenic variation, such as African trypanosome, is crucial for evading the immune system. Parasites release molecules that interfere with the normal function of the immune system.

retrieved_docs.title: Phagocytosis -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Since phagocytosis is a cell-mediated process involving the transport of a substance from the extracellular matrix into the cell, it is considered a form of endocytosis . Specifically, phagocytosis is a form of endocytosis that only involves the transport of larger particles (greater than 0.5 micrometers), such as bacteria or cellular debris. Phagocytosis is also described as a destructive endocytosis due to the fate of particles being endocytosed. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Granulation Tissue -- Cellular Level -- Immune Cells
retrieved_docs.content: Neutrophils (first to the wound) and macrophages are the primary immune cells at the site of wounds that aid in wound healing and protect against invading pathogens. Both cell types are phagocytic, meaning they engulf and ingest pathogens, debris, and damaged tissue, allowing for protection and healing. [1] [2] [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Alveolar Cells -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: Alveolar macrophages play an essential role in our immune system. They collect inhaled particles from the environment, such as coal, silica, and asbestos, and microbes, including viruses, bacteria, and fungi. Alveolar macrophages have a receptor named toll-like receptor, which binds to another receptor on the surface of microbial cells, the pathogen-associated molecular receptor. This interaction facilitates the phagocytosis of the pathogen and the secretion of pro-inflammatory cytokines to enhance the local immune response. Within the alveolar macrophage, engulfed microbes become fused with their lysosome to destroy the pathogen. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 1531
retrieved_docs.content: Viral antigens constitute the active ingredient of viral vaccines, providing the essential immunogenic component that stimulates the body’s immune response and confers protection against the targeted viral infections.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 1314
retrieved_docs.content: toxins, are employed to safely stimulate protective immune responses, and xenon, an inert gas with anesthetic properties, influences neuronal activity and cerebral physiology. Collectively, these diverse entities serve as important qualifiers for describing and investigating physiological mechanisms, disease processes, and therapeutic interventions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 3 -- Community 1759
retrieved_docs.content: Prophylactic vaccination or inoculation against certain bacterial diseases is classified as a therapeutic intervention and is a subcategory of the broader group termed Therapeutic Interventions: Immunization, Injections, Perfusion. This relationship indicates that preventive vaccination against bacterial pathogens is considered part of the overall therapeutic strategies that include various forms of immunization, injectable treatments, and perfusion techniques, highlighting its role within comprehensive patient care aimed at preventing infection.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 228
retrieved_docs.content: Finally, **Other puncture of artery** is placed under the parent category **Puncture of vessel**, reflecting its nature as a vascular access technique. The overarching class **Prophylactic vaccinations and inoculations** encompasses **Prophylactic vaccination or inoculation against certain bacterial diseases**, **human vaccination**, and the subset **التمنيع السلبي** (passive immunity), which denotes the transfer of pre‑formed antibodies for immediate, short‑term protection without active immune response generation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1825
retrieved_docs.content: Immunity disorders are classified as a subset of نقص غلوبيولين الدم, which refers to a deficiency of blood globulins. This globulin deficiency results in reduced levels of immunoglobulins, compromising the body’s immune defenses and predisposing individuals to infections and other immune‑related complications. Recognizing immunity disorders within the broader context of نقص غلوبيولين الدم helps clinicians understand the underlying immunological deficit and guides appropriate diagnostic evaluation and therapeutic strategies aimed at restoring adequate immune function.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2568
retrieved_docs.content: Prophylactic vaccination or inoculation against certain bacterial diseases is identified as a therapeutic intervention that includes immunization, injections, and perfusion methods. This specific preventive measure is placed under the broader category of prophylactic vaccinations and inoculations, indicating that it has a parent relationship to that general class. The overarching class of prophylactic vaccinations and inoculations functions to classify and organize these preventive immunization strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1795
retrieved_docs.content: The skin disorder حُمامَى is linked to other inflammatory conditions of the skin, indicating that it shares pathogenic mechanisms and clinical associations with additional inflammatory dermatoses. This “associated_with” relationship highlights the relevance of recognizing حُمامَى within the broader spectrum of skin inflammation for accurate diagnosis and comprehensive patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 2100
retrieved_docs.content: Injection or infusion of therapeutic or prophylactic substances and prophylactic vaccination or inoculation against certain bacterial diseases are both classified under the broader category of Therapeutic Interventions: Immunization, Injections, Perfusion. This parent category encompasses medical procedures that deliver therapeutic agents directly into the body, either through injection or infusion, as well as preventive measures such as bacterial vaccinations. By grouping these specific interventions within the same therapeutic framework, clinicians recognize that both the administration of therapeutic or prophylactic substances and the use of bacterial vaccines are integral components of immunization and perfusion strategies aimed at preventing disease and delivering treatment effectively.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 2041
retrieved_docs.content: Aplastic anemia, non‑regenerative anemia, arthritis, hemolytic anemia that is not caused by autoimmunity, and coronary artery inflammation are all classified within the broader category of immune disorders/autoimmune conditions. This grouping reflects the shared underlying involvement of the immune system in the pathogenesis of these diverse diseases, whether through direct autoimmune attack on blood‑forming tissues, inflammatory processes affecting joints, or vascular inflammation of the coronary arteries. Recognizing these entities as members of the immune/autoimmune disorder spectrum helps clinicians consider common immunologic mechanisms, guide diagnostic evaluation, and select therapeutic strategies that target immune dysregulation across these seemingly disparate clinical presentations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 3 -- Community 1539
retrieved_docs.content: Wiskott‑Aldrich anemia, aplastic anemia, arthritis, and cold‑type hemolytic anemia are all classified as members of the immune disorders/autoimmune (SMQ) category. By grouping these diverse conditions together, the classification highlights that each involves dysregulation of the immune system, whether through auto‑reactive mechanisms, immune‑mediated destruction of blood cells, or inflammatory joint disease. Recognizing these diseases as part of the broader autoimmune spectrum aids clinicians in understanding their shared pathophysiology, guiding diagnostic evaluation and informing therapeutic strategies that target immune pathways.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 4 -- Community 2778
retrieved_docs.content: that can disrupt cellular membranes and provoke immune reactions, while human insulin is the principal hormone governing carbohydrate metabolism and energy storage. Toxoids, which are inactivated toxins, are used to safely stimulate protective immune physiology without causing disease. Together, these diverse organisms, molecules, anatomical structures, and pharmacologic agents illustrate the wide range of factors that influence human physiological processes and underscore their importance in clinical assessment, diagnosis, and therapeutic intervention.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 1 -- Community 67
retrieved_docs.content: antitrichomonal agents are connected to hachimycin, indicating a therapeutic or mechanistic association. Collectively, the taxonomy highlights that antiprotozoal agents are employed against parasites and single‑cell infections and have therapeutic relevance across bacterial, fungal, protozoal and parasitic diseases, providing a structured framework for clinical decision‑making and drug development.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 4 -- Community 2721
retrieved_docs.content: Amphotericin B is identified as a therapeutic agent that may be used to treat diseases of white blood cells, indicating its potential role in managing conditions that affect the immune cell population.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 501
retrieved_docs.content: Meningitis, an inflammation of the protective membranes covering the brain and spinal cord, encompasses several specific forms, including bacterial meningitis (التهاب السحايا الجرثومي), which represents a distinct subtype caused by bacterial infection. Recognizing bacterial meningitis as a particular category within the broader condition of meningitis is essential for accurate diagnosis, targeted antimicrobial therapy, and effective patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1591
retrieved_docs.content: Immunization for autoimmune disease encompasses human vaccination as a specific form of immunization, indicating that vaccines administered to humans can be used to prevent or modulate autoimmune conditions. Human vaccination is situated within the broader category of prophylactic vaccinations and inoculations, reflecting its role as a preventive intervention designed to protect individuals from infectious agents. Prophylactic vaccinations and inoculations functions as the overarching classification that groups together all preventive vaccine strategies, including human vaccination, and thereby provides the hierarchical framework linking general preventive immunization concepts to the more specific application of immunization for autoimmune disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 4 -- Community 2732
retrieved_docs.content: Demethylchlortetracyklin, a tetracycline‑derived antimicrobial agent, may be used to prevent عدوى وحيدات الخلية (infections caused by single‑celled organisms). This relationship underscores the potential prophylactic role of demethylchlortetracyklin in managing unicellular infections.
